concepts.lesson: A random experiment is one in which the outcome cannot be predicted with certainty, even though all possible outcomes are known. Such experiments form the basis of probability theory.
The sample space is the set of all possible outcomes of a random experiment. It is usually denoted by S and includes every outcome that may occur.
concepts.real_example: Sample space of a coin toss.

concepts.lesson: An event is a subset of the sample space and consists of one or more outcomes. Events can be simple or compound depending on the number of outcomes involved.
concepts.real_example: Getting an even number on a die.

concepts.lesson: The probability of an event is defined as the ratio of favourable outcomes to total possible outcomes, assuming all outcomes are equally likely.
The complement of an event consists of all outcomes that do not favour the event. The probability of the complement is equal to one minus the probability of the event.
